explicit_text: Chiwapa Bottom was a low area where my grandfather settled after coming down from Indiana. He bought land cheaply there, cut the big timber, and shipped it back to Indiana for Taylor Pearson's Lumber Company. My grandfather ended up owning a lot of land in that area and stayed in Mississippi because of it.

explicit_text: Indiana is where my grandfather originally came from before moving to Mississippi. Indiana was the family’s original home, and it was connected to our lumber business.

explicit_text: My mother was Hermian Buskirk, born Hermian Pope, granddaughter of a country doctor. She grew up with stories of her mother driving her father during emergency calls at night. Hermian was a quiet and humble woman who balanced my father’s outspoken nature.

explicit_text: My father, Bob Buskirk, was deeply involved in the United Methodist Church before he was called to preach. He served as Superintendent of Sunday school, taught classes, was on the board, and chaired the board at one time. The church was central to our family’s life and his ministry.

explicit_text: Jesus was central to my father’s calling and ministry. My dad believed strongly in the Scripture about Jesus giving apostles and pastors to equip the saints. His call to preach was deeply connected to his faith in Jesus and his desire to be a voice for Him.

explicit_text: Grover Meadows was a man my father played baseball with who later became a minister. When my dad stopped going to church for three years, Grover came to our home and invited us to a revival at Pleasant Grove. His sermon and encouragement led my father to accept his call to preach.

explicit_text: Bob Buskirk was my father, born in 1903, the middle of five sons. He was called to preach at eighteen while standing over his mother’s grave but delayed accepting it. Eventually, after a period of struggle and encouragement from Grover Meadows, he embraced his ministry and served in several Mississippi communities.

explicit_text: I was named James Bradford Buskirk after my mother’s father and grew up being called Brad. It wasn’t until college, influenced by an English teacher, that I started going by James and then Jimmy. My name reflects my family heritage and my personal journey.

explicit_text: I joined the Scouts when I was thirteen after an incident where the assistant Scoutmaster caught me playing a prank. Becoming a Scout helped me overcome my timidity and develop public speaking and leadership skills. I eventually became senior patrol leader and learned many lessons that prepared me for ministry.
